sections.heading: Learn with Courses
sections.body:
To learn more about using facets in MongoDB Search, take Unit 9 of the
[Intro To MongoDB Course on MongoDB University](https://learn.mongodb.com/courses/mongodb-atlas-search). The 1.5 hour unit includes an overview
of MongoDB Search and lessons on creating MongoDB Search indexes, running
[$search](pipe.$search) queries using compound operators,
and grouping results by using `facet`.

sections.heading: Learn by Watching
sections.body:
Follow along with this video to learn about how you can create and use a
numeric and string [faceted navigation](fts-facet-ref) in your query to group results
and retrieve a count of the results in the groups.

*Duration: 11 Minutes*

[https://youtu.be/90icEZ8fzJE](https://youtu.be/90icEZ8fzJE)

This tutorial describes how to create an index with a [facet definition](fts-facet-definition) on string, date, and numeric fields
in the `sample_mflix.movies` collection. It shows how to run a MongoDB Search
query against those fields for results grouped by values for the string
field and by ranges for the date and numeric fields, including the
count for each of those groups. It takes you through the following
steps:

- Set up a MongoDB Search index with facet definition on the `genres`,
  `released`, and `year` fields in the `sample_mflix.movies`
  collection.

- Run a MongoDB Search query against the `released` field in the
  `sample_mflix.movies` collection for results grouped by values for
  the `genres` field and by ranges for the `year` field.

To complete these tutorials, in addition to the prerequisites listed in
the [Prerequisites](fts-design-patterns-prereqs), you must have a MongoDB
instance running MongoDB 7.0 or later.

In this section, you will create a MongoDB Search index on the `genres`,
`year`, and `released` fields in the `sample_mflix.movies`
collection.

sections.heading: Step 1: Connect to your cluster using .
sections.body:
Open [Compass](https://www.mongodb.com/try/download/compass) and connect to your cluster. For
detailed instructions, see [Compass](atlas-connect-via-compass).

sections.heading: Step 2: Specify the database and collection.
sections.body:
On the Database screen, click the name of the database
sample\_mflix, then click the name of the collection movies.

sections.heading: Step 3: Create the  index.
sections.body:
- Click the Indexes tab, then select Search Indexes.

- Click Create Atlas Search Index to open the index creation dialog box.

- Name the index `facet-tutorial`.

- Specify the JSON (Javascript Object Notation) MongoDB Search index definition.

  ```json
  {
    "mappings": { 
      "dynamic": true, 
      "fields": { 
          "genres": {
              "type": "token"
          }
      } 
    }
  }
  ```

- Click Create Search Index.

You can use [faceted navigation](fts-facet-ref) in queries that use the
[$search](pipe.$search) and [$searchMeta](pipe.$searchMeta) stages. In this
section, you will connect to your cluster and the run the sample query
against the `sample_mflix.movies` collection using the
[$searchMeta](pipe.$searchMeta) or [$search](pipe.$search) stage to group the
`genre` and `year` fields into buckets. To optimize performance:

- Use [$searchMeta](pipe.$searchMeta) stage if you only need the `facet` metadata.

- Use [$search](pipe.$search) stage if you want to retrieve both the query
  results and the `facet` metadata.

Open MongoDB Compass and connect to your cluster. For detailed
instructions on connecting, see [Compass](atlas-connect-via-compass).

On the Database screen, click the `sample_mflix`
database, then click the `movies` collection.

The query uses the following `searchMeta` operator clauses:

- [near](near-ref) to search for movies released with a
  `pivot` distance from  `origin` of approximately three months
  from November 11, 1921

- `facets` to request the number of movies in each genre in the
  `genres` string array field and the number of movies in the years
  1910 to 1939, inclusive

To run this query in MongoDB Compass:

- Click the Aggregations tab.

- Click Select..., then configure each of the following
  pipeline stages by selecting the stage from the dropdown and adding
  the query for that stage. Click Add Stage to add
  additional stages.

  You can run this query using [$searchMeta](pipe.$searchMeta) or using
  [$search](pipe.$search) with the `SEARCH_META` aggregation variable.

  $searchMeta

  | Pipeline Stage | Query                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       |
  | -------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
  | $searchMeta    | \{ "index": "facet-tutorial", "facet": \{ "operator": \{ "near": \{ "path": "released", "origin": ISODate("1921-11-01T00:00:00.000+00:00"), "pivot": 7776000000 } }, "facets": \{ "genresFacet": \{ "type": "string", "path": "genres" }, "yearFacet": \{ "type": "number", "path": "year", "boundaries": \[1910, 1920, 1930, 1940] } } } } |

  $search with $$SEARCH_META

  | Pipeline Stage | Query                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     |
  | -------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
  | $search        | \{ "index": "facet-tutorial", "facet": \{ "operator": \{ "near": \{ "path": "released", "origin": ISODate("1921-11-01T00:00:00.000+00:00"), "pivot": 7776000000 } }, "facets": \{ "genresFacet": \{ "type": "string", "path": "genres" }, "yearFacet": \{ "type": "number", "path": "year", "boundaries" : \[1910,1920,1930,1940] } } } } |
  | $facet         | \{ meta: \[ \{ $replaceWith: "$$SEARCH\_META" }, \{ $limit: 1 } ] }                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       |
  | $set           | \{ meta: \{ $arrayElemAt: \["$meta", 0] } }                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                               |

  If you enabled Auto Preview, MongoDB Compass displays the
  following documents next to the `$set` pipeline stage:

  ```javascript
  count: Object
      lowerBound: 20878
      facet: Object
      genresFacet: Object
          buckets: Array (10)
          0: Object
              _id: "Drama"
              count: 12149
          1: Object
              _id: "Comedy"
              count: 6436
          2: Object
              _id: "Romance"
              count: 3274
          3: Object
              _id: "Crime"
              count: 2429
          4: Object
              _id: "Thriller"
              count: 2400
          5: Object
              _id: "Action"
              count: 2349
          6: Object
              _id: "Adventure"
              count: 1876
          7: Object
              _id: "Documentary"
              count: 1755
          8: Object
              _id: "Horror"
              count: 1432
          9: Object
              _id: "Biography"
              count: 1244
      yearFacet: Object
          buckets: Array (3)
          0: Object
              _id: 1910
              count: 14
          1: Object
              _id: 1920
              count: 47
          2: Object
              _id: 1930
              count: 238
  ```

The results show metadata results for two types of facet search. The
`genresFacet` document shows the number of movies in each genre and
the `yearFacet` document shows a count of the number of movies
within the boundaries:

- `1910-1920`, inclusive lower bound for the `1910` bucket and exclusive upper bound for the `1920` bucket

- `1920-1930`, inclusive lower bound for the `1920` bucket and exclusive
  upper bound for the `1930` bucket

- `1930-1940`, inclusive lower bound for the `1930` bucket and exclusive
  upper bound for the `1940` bucket

You can learn more about the [faceted navigation](fts-facet-ref) in MongoDB Search with the following
course or video.

sections.heading: Step 1: Set up the C project.
sections.body:
In your terminal, navigate to where you want to create your application,
then run the following command to create a directory called
`atlas-search-project` for this project:

```shell
# Create a new directory for the project
mkdir atlas-search-project && cd atlas-search-project
```

Add the C driver to your project by following the instructions in the
[MongoDB C Driver documentation](languages/c/c-driver/current/get-started).

codeBlocks.content:
# Create a new directory for the project
mkdir atlas-search-project && cd atlas-search-project

sections.heading: Step 2: Define the index.
Step 3: Set up a CMake application
sections.body:
To configure your application, create a `CMakeLists.txt` file in
your project directory. Then, add the following code to the file:

```txt
cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 3.11)
project(atlas-search-index LANGUAGES C)
add_executable (query.out facet_query.c)
find_package(mongoc <version> REQUIRED)
target_link_libraries(query.out mongoc::mongoc)
```

The preceding code performs the following actions:

- Configures a C project.

- Creates a `index.out` executable for your application.

- Finds and requires the C driver. Replace the `<version>`
  placeholder with your C driver version facet, such as `2.0.0`.

- Links the program to the `libmongoc` library.

> **Note:** In the sample `CMakeLists.txt` file, the `mongoc::mongoc` target
> points to either the static library or the shared library.
> The library type depends on which one is available and
> whichever type the user specifies in the `MONGOC_DEFAULT_IMPORTED_LIBRARY_TYPE`
> CMake configuration setting. If you don't set this value and
> both library types are available, `mongoc::mongoc` uses
> the static library.
>
> You can use the `mongoc::static` target to explicitly use the
> static library or the `mongoc::shared` target to use the shared
> library.

codeBlocks.content:
cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 3.11)
project(atlas-search-index LANGUAGES C)
add_executable (query.out facet_query.c)
find_package(mongoc <version> REQUIRED)
target_link_libraries(query.out mongoc::mongoc)

sections.heading: Step 1: Set up your Java project with the MongoDB Java driver.
sections.body:
In your IDE, create a new [Maven](https://maven.apache.org/) or
[Gradle](https://gradle.org/) project. Add the Bill of
Materials (BOM) for MongoDB JVM artifacts to your project to
organize dependency versions. The BOM simplifies dependency
management by ensuring that you maintain consistent and compatible
versions of dependencies, such as between the Java driver and
the core driver library. Use the BOM to avoid version conflicts
and simplify upgrades.

Select from the following **Maven** and **Gradle** tabs
to view instructions for adding the BOM for each dependency manager:

Maven

Add the following code to the `dependencyManagement` list in your
`pom.xml` file:

```xml
<dependencyManagement>
    <dependencies>
        <dependency>
            <groupId>org.mongodb</groupId>
            <artifactId>mongodb-driver-bom</artifactId>
            <version>5.5.1</version>
            <type>pom</type>
            <scope>import</scope>
        </dependency>
    </dependencies>
</dependencyManagement>
```

Gradle

Add the following code to dependencies list in your
`build.gradle` file:

```groovy
dependencies {
    implementation(platform("org.mongodb:mongodb-driver-bom:5.5.1"))
}
```

To view a list of dependencies that the BOM manages, see
the [mongodb-driver-bom dependency listing](https://mvnrepository.com/artifact/org.mongodb/mongodb-driver-bom/5.51)
on the Maven Repository website.

After adding the BOM, select from the following **Maven** and **Gradle** tabs to view
instructions for adding the MongoDB Java Sync Driver as a dependency
in your project:

Maven

Add the following code to the `dependencies` array in your Maven
project's `pom.xml` file:

```xml
<dependencies>
    <dependency>    
      <groupId>org.mongodb</groupId>    
      <artifactId>mongodb-driver-sync</artifactId>    
      <version>4.11.1</version>    
    </dependency>
</dependencies>

```

Gradle

Add the following to the `dependencies` array in your Gradle
project's `build.gradle` file:

```json
dependencies {
   // MongoDB Java Sync Driver v4.11.0 or later
   implementation 'org.mongodb:mongodb-driver-sync'
}

```

Run your package manager to install the dependencies to your project.

For more detailed installation instructions and version compatibility, see

sections.heading: Step 1: Set up your Kotlin project with the MongoDB Kotlin Coroutine driver.
sections.body:
In your IDE, create a new [Maven](https://maven.apache.org/) or
[Gradle](https://gradle.org/) project. Add the Bill of
Materials (BOM) for MongoDB JVM artifacts to your project to
organize dependency versions. The BOM simplifies dependency
management by ensuring that you maintain consistent and compatible
versions of dependencies, such as between the Java driver and
the core driver library. Use the BOM to avoid version conflicts
and simplify upgrades.

Select from the following **Maven** and **Gradle** tabs
to view instructions for adding the BOM for each dependency manager:

Maven

Add the following code to the `dependencyManagement` list in your
`pom.xml` file:

```xml
<dependencyManagement>
    <dependencies>
        <dependency>
            <groupId>org.mongodb</groupId>
            <artifactId>mongodb-driver-bom</artifactId>
            <version>5.5.1</version>
            <type>pom</type>
            <scope>import</scope>
        </dependency>
    </dependencies>
</dependencyManagement>
```

Gradle

Add the following code to the dependencies list in your
`build.gradle` file:

```groovy
dependencies {
    implementation(platform("org.mongodb:mongodb-driver-bom:5.5.1"))
}
```

To view a list of dependencies that the BOM manages, see
the [mongodb-driver-bom dependency listing](https://mvnrepository.com/artifact/org.mongodb/mongodb-driver-bom/5.51)
on the Maven Repository website.

After adding the BOM, select from the following **Maven** and **Gradle** tabs to view
instructions for adding the MongoDB Kotlin Coroutine Driver as a dependency
in your project:

Maven

Add the following code to the `dependencies` array in your Maven
project's `pom.xml` file:

```xml
<dependencies>
    <dependency>
        <groupId>org.mongodb</groupId>
        <artifactId>mongodb-driver-kotlin-coroutine</artifactId>
    </dependency>
</dependencies>
```

Gradle

Add the following to the `dependencies` array in your Gradle
project's `build.gradle` file:

```json
dependencies {
    implementation("org.mongodb:mongodb-driver-kotlin-coroutine")
}
```

Run your package manager to install the dependencies to your project.

For more detailed installation instructions and version compatibility, see
the [MongoDB Kotlin Coroutine Driver documentation](kotlin/coroutine/current/quick-start).

sections.heading: Step 2: Define the index.
sections.body:
In your project's base package directory, create a
`CreateIndex.kt` file and copy and paste the following code
into this file.

```kotlin
import com.mongodb.kotlin.client.coroutine.MongoClient
import kotlinx.coroutines.runBlocking
import org.bson.Document

fun main() = runBlocking {
    // connect to your Atlas cluster
    val uri = "<connection-string>"
    
    val mongoClient = MongoClient.create(uri)
    try {
        // set namespace
        val database = mongoClient.getDatabase("sample_mflix")
        val collection = database.getCollection<Document>("movies")
        val indexName = "facet-tutorial"
        
        val genresField = Document("type", "token")
        
        val index = Document("mappings", 
            Document("dynamic", true)
                .append("fields", Document("genres", genresField)))
        
        val result = collection.createSearchIndex(indexName, index)
        println("New index name: $result")
    } finally {
        mongoClient.close()
    }
}
```

Replace `<connection-string>` with the connection string for your
Atlas cluster or local Atlas deployment.

Atlas Cluster

Your connection string should use the following format:

```
mongodb+srv://<db_username>:<db_password>@<clusterName>.<hostname>.mongodb.net
```

To learn more, see [Driver](connect-via-driver).

Local or Self-Managed

Your connection string should use the
following format:

```
mongodb://localhost:<port-number>/?directConnection=true
```

To learn more, see [Connection Strings](manual/reference/connection-string).

sections.heading: Step 3: Compile and run the file to create the index.
sections.body:
Compile and run your application in your IDE or your shell.

**Input:**

```shell
kotlinc CreateIndex.kt -include-runtime -d CreateIndex.jar
java -jar CreateIndex.jar
```

**Output:**

```
New index name: facet-tutorial
```

You can use [faceted navigation](fts-facet-ref) in queries that use the
[$search](pipe.$search) and [$searchMeta](pipe.$searchMeta) stages. In this
section, you will connect to your cluster and the run the sample query
against the `sample_mflix.movies` collection using the
[$searchMeta](pipe.$searchMeta) or [$search](pipe.$search) stage to group the
`genre` and `year` fields into buckets. To optimize performance:

- Use [$searchMeta](pipe.$searchMeta) stage if you only need the `facet` metadata.

- Use [$search](pipe.$search) stage if you want to retrieve both the query
  results and the `facet` metadata.

| mongodb-driver-kotlin-coroutine | 4.10.0 or higher version |
| ------------------------------- | ------------------------ |

- Create a file named `FacetQuery.kt`.

- Copy and paste the following code into the `FacetQuery.kt` file.

  The code example performs the following tasks:

  - Imports `mongodb` packages and dependencies.

  - Establishes a connection to your cluster.

  - Uses the following to query the collection:

    - [near](near-ref) to search for movies released with a
      `pivot` distance from  `origin` of approximately three months
      from November 11, 1921

    - `facets` to request the number of movies in each genre in the
      `genres` string array field and the number of movies in the years
      1910 to 1939, inclusive

  - Prints the documents that match the query from the `AggregateFlow` instance.

  You can run this query using [$searchMeta](pipe.$searchMeta) or using
  [$search](pipe.$search) with the `SEARCH_META` aggregation variable.

  $searchMeta

  ```kotlin
  import com.mongodb.kotlin.client.coroutine.MongoClient
  import kotlinx.coroutines.runBlocking
  import org.bson.Document
  import java.time.Instant
  import java.util.*

  fun main() {
      // connect to your Atlas cluster
      val uri = "<connection-string>"
      val mongoClient = MongoClient.create(uri)
      
      // set namespace
      val database = mongoClient.getDatabase("sample_mflix")
      val collection = database.getCollection<Document>("movies")

      runBlocking {
          // define pipeline
          val agg = Document(
              "\$searchMeta",
              Document("index", "facet-tutorial")
                  .append(
                      "facet",
                      Document(
                          "operator",
                          Document(
                              "near",
                              Document("path", "released")
                                  .append("origin", Date.from(Instant.parse("1921-11-01T00:00:00.000+00:00")))
                                  .append("pivot", 7776000000L)
                          )
                      )
                          .append(
                              "facets",
                              Document(
                                  "genresFacet",
                                  Document("type", "string").append("path", "genres")
                              )
                                  .append(
                                      "yearFacet",
                                      Document("type", "number").append("path", "year")
                                          .append("boundaries", listOf(1910, 1920, 1930, 1940))
                                  )
                          )
                  )
          )

          // run pipeline and print results
          val resultsFlow = collection.aggregate<Document>(
              listOf(agg)
          )
          resultsFlow.collect { println(it) }
      }
      mongoClient.close()
  }


  ```

  $search with $$SEARCH_META

  ```kotlin
  import com.mongodb.kotlin.client.coroutine.MongoClient
  import kotlinx.coroutines.flow.collect
  import kotlinx.coroutines.runBlocking
  import org.bson.Document
  import java.time.Instant
  import java.util.Date

  fun main() {
      // Connection URI for your MongoDB Atlas cluster
      val uri = "<connection-string>"
      val mongoClient = MongoClient.create(uri)

      // Set namespace (database and collection)
      val database = mongoClient.getDatabase("sample_mflix")
      val collection = database.getCollection<Document>("movies")

      runBlocking {
          // Define the aggregation pipeline
          val searchStage = Document("\$search", Document("index", "facet-tutorial")
              .append("facet", Document("operator", Document("near", Document("path", "released")
                  // Replace +00:00 with Z
                  .append("origin", Date.from(Instant.parse("1921-11-01T00:00:00.000Z"))) 
                  .append("pivot", 7776000000L)))
                  .append("facets", Document("genresFacet", Document("type", "string")
                      .append("path", "genres"))
                      .append("yearFacet", Document("type", "number").append("path", "year")
                          .append("boundaries", listOf(1910, 1920, 1930, 1940))))))

          val facetStage = Document("\$facet", Document("meta", listOf(
              Document("\$replaceWith", "\$\$SEARCH_META"),
              Document("\$limit", 1)
          )))

          val setStage = Document("\$set", Document("meta", Document("\$arrayElemAt", listOf("\$meta", 0))))

          // Run the aggregation pipeline and print results
          val resultsFlow = collection.aggregate<Document>(
              listOf(searchStage, facetStage, setStage)
          )

          resultsFlow.collect { println(it.toJson()) } // Convert each result to JSON and print
      }

      // Close the MongoDB client
      mongoClient.close()
  }

  ```

- Before you run the sample, replace `<connection-string>` with your
  Atlas connection string. Ensure that your connection string
  includes your database user's credentials. To learn more, see
  [Driver](connect-via-driver).

When you run the `FacetQuery.kt` program in your IDE, it prints
a result similar to the following:

```none
{"meta": {
   "count": {"lowerBound": 20878},
   "facet": {
     "genresFacet": {
       "buckets": [
         {"_id": "Drama", "count": 12149},
         {"_id": "Comedy", "count": 6436},
         {"_id": "Romance", "count": 3274},
         {"_id": "Crime", "count": 2429},
         {"_id": "Thriller", "count": 2400},
         {"_id": "Action", "count": 2349},
         {"_id": "Adventure", "count": 1876},
         {"_id": "Documentary", "count": 1755},
         {"_id": "Horror", "count": 1432},
         {"_id": "Biography", "count": 1244}
       ]
     },
     "yearFacet": {
       "buckets": [
         {"_id": 1910, "count": 14},
         {"_id": 1920, "count": 47},
         {"_id": 1930, "count": 238}
       ]
     }
   }
 }}
 }
}
```

The results show metadata results for two types of facet search. The
`genresFacet` document shows the number of movies in each genre and
the `yearFacet` document shows a count of the number of movies
within the boundaries:

- `1910-1920`, inclusive lower bound for the `1910` bucket and exclusive upper bound for the `1920` bucket

- `1920-1930`, inclusive lower bound for the `1920` bucket and exclusive
  upper bound for the `1930` bucket

- `1930-1940`, inclusive lower bound for the `1930` bucket and exclusive
  upper bound for the `1940` bucket

You can learn more about the [faceted navigation](fts-facet-ref) in MongoDB Search with the following
course or video.

sections.heading: Step 2: Define the index.
sections.body:
Create a `create_index.py` file in your project directory,
and copy and paste the following code into the file.

```python
from pymongo import MongoClient
from pymongo.operations import SearchIndexModel

# connect to your Atlas deployment
uri = "<connection-string>"
client = MongoClient(uri)

# set namespace
database = client["sample_mflix"]
collection = database["movies"]

# define your MongoDB Search index
search_index_model = SearchIndexModel(
    definition={
        "mappings": {
            "dynamic": True,
            "fields": {
                "genres": {
                    "type": "token"
                }
            }
        },
    },
    name="facet-tutorial",
)

# create the index
result = collection.create_search_index(model=search_index_model)
print(f"New index name: {result}")
```

Replace `<connection-string>` with the connection string for your
Atlas cluster or local Atlas deployment.

Atlas Cluster

sections.heading: Step 3: Create the  index.
sections.body:
- Click the Indexes tab, then select Search Indexes.

- Click Create Atlas Search Index to open the index creation dialog box.

- Name the index `facet-tutorial`.

- Specify the JSON (Javascript Object Notation) MongoDB Search index definition.

  ```json
  {
    "mappings": { 
      "dynamic": true, 
      "fields": { 
          "genres": {
              "type": "token"
          }
      } 
    }
  }
  ```

- Click Create Search Index.

You can use [faceted navigation](fts-facet-ref) in queries that use the
[$search](pipe.$search) and [$searchMeta](pipe.$searchMeta) stages. In this
section, you will connect to your cluster and the run the sample query
against the `sample_mflix.movies` collection using the
[$searchMeta](pipe.$searchMeta) or [$search](pipe.$search) stage to group the
`genre` and `year` fields into buckets. To optimize performance:

- Use [$searchMeta](pipe.$searchMeta) stage if you only need the `facet` metadata.

- Use [$search](pipe.$search) stage if you want to retrieve both the query
  results and the `facet` metadata.

Open MongoDB Compass and connect to your cluster. For detailed
instructions on connecting, see [Compass](atlas-connect-via-compass).

On the Database screen, click the `sample_mflix`
database, then click the `movies` collection.

The query uses the following `searchMeta` operator clauses:

- [near](near-ref) to search for movies released with a
  `pivot` distance from  `origin` of approximately three months
  from November 11, 1921

- `facets` to request the number of movies in each genre in the
  `genres` string array field and the number of movies in the years
  1910 to 1939, inclusive

To run this query in MongoDB Compass:

- Click the Aggregations tab.

- Click Select..., then configure each of the following
  pipeline stages by selecting the stage from the dropdown and adding
  the query for that stage. Click Add Stage to add
  additional stages.

  You can run this query using [$searchMeta](pipe.$searchMeta) or using
  [$search](pipe.$search) with the `SEARCH_META` aggregation variable.

  $searchMeta

  | Pipeline Stage | Query                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       |
  | -------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
  | $searchMeta    | \{ "index": "facet-tutorial", "facet": \{ "operator": \{ "near": \{ "path": "released", "origin": ISODate("1921-11-01T00:00:00.000+00:00"), "pivot": 7776000000 } }, "facets": \{ "genresFacet": \{ "type": "string", "path": "genres" }, "yearFacet": \{ "type": "number", "path": "year", "boundaries": \[1910, 1920, 1930, 1940] } } } } |

  $search with $$SEARCH_META

  | Pipeline Stage | Query                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     |
  | -------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
  | $search        | \{ "index": "facet-tutorial", "facet": \{ "operator": \{ "near": \{ "path": "released", "origin": ISODate("1921-11-01T00:00:00.000+00:00"), "pivot": 7776000000 } }, "facets": \{ "genresFacet": \{ "type": "string", "path": "genres" }, "yearFacet": \{ "type": "number", "path": "year", "boundaries" : \[1910,1920,1930,1940] } } } } |
  | $facet         | \{ meta: \[ \{ $replaceWith: "$$SEARCH\_META" }, \{ $limit: 1 } ] }                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       |
  | $set           | \{ meta: \{ $arrayElemAt: \["$meta", 0] } }                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                               |

  If you enabled Auto Preview, MongoDB Compass displays the
  following documents next to the `$set` pipeline stage:

              count: 1432

              _id: 1930

The results show metadata results for two types of facet search. The
`genresFacet` document shows the number of movies in each genre and
the `yearFacet` document shows a count of the number of movies
within the boundaries:

- `1910-1920`, inclusive lower bound for the `1910` bucket and exclusive upper bound for the `1920` bucket

  upper bound for the `1940` bucket

sections.heading: Step 2: Define the index.
Step 3: Set up a CMake application
sections.body:
To configure your application, create a `CMakeLists.txt` file in
your project directory. Then, add the following code to the file:

```txt
cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 3.11)
project(atlas-search-index LANGUAGES C)
add_executable (query.out facet_query.c)
find_package(mongoc <version> REQUIRED)
target_link_libraries(query.out mongoc::mongoc)
```

The preceding code performs the following actions:

- Configures a C project.

- Creates a `index.out` executable for your application.

- Finds and requires the C driver. Replace the `<version>`
  placeholder with your C driver version facet, such as `2.0.0`.

- Links the program to the `libmongoc` library.

> **Note:** In the sample `CMakeLists.txt` file, the `mongoc::mongoc` target
> points to either the static library or the shared library.
> The library type depends on which one is available and
> whichever type the user specifies in the `MONGOC_DEFAULT_IMPORTED_LIBRARY_TYPE`
> CMake configuration setting. If you don't set this value and
> both library types are available, `mongoc::mongoc` uses
> the static library.
>
> You can use the `mongoc::static` target to explicitly use the
> static library or the `mongoc::shared` target to use the shared
> library.

cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 3.11)
project(atlas-search-index LANGUAGES C)
add_executable (query.out facet_query.c)
find_package(mongoc <version> REQUIRED)
target_link_libraries(query.out mongoc::mongoc)

sections.heading: Step 1: Set up your Java project with the MongoDB Java driver.
sections.body:
In your IDE, create a new [Maven](https://maven.apache.org/) or
[Gradle](https://gradle.org/) project. Add the Bill of
Materials (BOM) for MongoDB JVM artifacts to your project to
organize dependency versions. The BOM simplifies dependency
management by ensuring that you maintain consistent and compatible
versions of dependencies, such as between the Java driver and
the core driver library. Use the BOM to avoid version conflicts
and simplify upgrades.

Select from the following **Maven** and **Gradle** tabs
to view instructions for adding the BOM for each dependency manager:

Maven

Add the following code to the `dependencyManagement` list in your
`pom.xml` file:

```xml
<dependencyManagement>
    <dependencies>
        <dependency>
            <groupId>org.mongodb</groupId>
            <artifactId>mongodb-driver-bom</artifactId>
            <version>5.5.1</version>
            <type>pom</type>
            <scope>import</scope>
        </dependency>
    </dependencies>
</dependencyManagement>
```

Gradle

Add the following code to dependencies list in your
`build.gradle` file:

```groovy
dependencies {
    implementation(platform("org.mongodb:mongodb-driver-bom:5.5.1"))
}
```

To view a list of dependencies that the BOM manages, see
the [mongodb-driver-bom dependency listing](https://mvnrepository.com/artifact/org.mongodb/mongodb-driver-bom/5.51)
on the Maven Repository website.

After adding the BOM, select from the following **Maven** and **Gradle** tabs to view
instructions for adding the MongoDB Java Sync Driver as a dependency
in your project:

Maven

Add the following code to the `dependencies` array in your Maven
project's `pom.xml` file:

```xml
<dependencies>
    <dependency>    
      <groupId>org.mongodb</groupId>    
      <artifactId>mongodb-driver-sync</artifactId>    
      <version>4.11.1</version>    
    </dependency>
</dependencies>

```

Gradle

Add the following to the `dependencies` array in your Gradle
project's `build.gradle` file:

```json
dependencies {
   // MongoDB Java Sync Driver v4.11.0 or later
   implementation 'org.mongodb:mongodb-driver-sync'
}

```

Run your package manager to install the dependencies to your project.

For more detailed installation instructions and version compatibility, see
the [MongoDB Java Driver documentation](java/sync/current/get-started).

sections.heading: Step 2: Define the index.
sections.body:
In your project's base package directory, create a
`CreateIndex.kt` file and copy and paste the following code
into this file.

```kotlin
import com.mongodb.kotlin.client.coroutine.MongoClient
import kotlinx.coroutines.runBlocking
import org.bson.Document

fun main() = runBlocking {
    // connect to your Atlas cluster
    val uri = "<connection-string>"
    
    val mongoClient = MongoClient.create(uri)
    try {
        // set namespace
        val database = mongoClient.getDatabase("sample_mflix")
        val collection = database.getCollection<Document>("movies")
        val indexName = "facet-tutorial"
        
        val genresField = Document("type", "token")
        
        val index = Document("mappings", 
            Document("dynamic", true)
                .append("fields", Document("genres", genresField)))
        
        val result = collection.createSearchIndex(indexName, index)
        println("New index name: $result")
    } finally {
        mongoClient.close()
    }
}
```

Replace `<connection-string>` with the connection string for your
Atlas cluster or local Atlas deployment.

Atlas Cluster

Your connection string should use the following format:

```
mongodb+srv://<db_username>:<db_password>@<clusterName>.<hostname>.mongodb.net
```

To learn more, see [Driver](connect-via-driver).

Local or Self-Managed

Your connection string should use the
following format:

```
mongodb://localhost:<port-number>/?directConnection=true
```

To learn more, see [Connection Strings](manual/reference/connection-string).

sections.heading: Step 3: Compile and run the file to create the index.
sections.body:
Compile and run your application in your IDE or your shell.

**Input:**

```shell
kotlinc CreateIndex.kt -include-runtime -d CreateIndex.jar
java -jar CreateIndex.jar
```

**Output:**

```
New index name: facet-tutorial
```

You can use [faceted navigation](fts-facet-ref) in queries that use the
[$search](pipe.$search) and [$searchMeta](pipe.$searchMeta) stages. In this
section, you will connect to your cluster and the run the sample query
against the `sample_mflix.movies` collection using the
[$searchMeta](pipe.$searchMeta) or [$search](pipe.$search) stage to group the
`genre` and `year` fields into buckets. To optimize performance:

- Use [$searchMeta](pipe.$searchMeta) stage if you only need the `facet` metadata.

- Use [$search](pipe.$search) stage if you want to retrieve both the query
  results and the `facet` metadata.

| mongodb-driver-kotlin-coroutine | 4.10.0 or higher version |
| ------------------------------- | ------------------------ |

- Create a file named `FacetQuery.kt`.

- Copy and paste the following code into the `FacetQuery.kt` file.

  The code example performs the following tasks:

  - Imports `mongodb` packages and dependencies.

  - Establishes a connection to your cluster.

  - Uses the following to query the collection:

    - [near](near-ref) to search for movies released with a
      `pivot` distance from  `origin` of approximately three months
      from November 11, 1921

    - `facets` to request the number of movies in each genre in the
      `genres` string array field and the number of movies in the years
      1910 to 1939, inclusive

  - Prints the documents that match the query from the `AggregateFlow` instance.

  You can run this query using [$searchMeta](pipe.$searchMeta) or using
  [$search](pipe.$search) with the `SEARCH_META` aggregation variable.

  $searchMeta

  ```kotlin
  import com.mongodb.kotlin.client.coroutine.MongoClient
  import kotlinx.coroutines.runBlocking
  import org.bson.Document
  import java.time.Instant
  import java.util.*

  fun main() {
      // connect to your Atlas cluster
      val uri = "<connection-string>"
      val mongoClient = MongoClient.create(uri)
      
      // set namespace
      val database = mongoClient.getDatabase("sample_mflix")
      val collection = database.getCollection<Document>("movies")

      runBlocking {
          // define pipeline
          val agg = Document(
              "\$searchMeta",
              Document("index", "facet-tutorial")
                  .append(
                      "facet",
                      Document(
                          "operator",
                          Document(
                              "near",
                              Document("path", "released")
                                  .append("origin", Date.from(Instant.parse("1921-11-01T00:00:00.000+00:00")))
                                  .append("pivot", 7776000000L)
                          )
                      )
                          .append(
                              "facets",
                              Document(
                                  "genresFacet",
                                  Document("type", "string").append("path", "genres")
                              )
                                  .append(
                                      "yearFacet",
                                      Document("type", "number").append("path", "year")
                                          .append("boundaries", listOf(1910, 1920, 1930, 1940))
                                  )
                          )
                  )
          )

          // run pipeline and print results
          val resultsFlow = collection.aggregate<Document>(
              listOf(agg)
          )
          resultsFlow.collect { println(it) }
      }
      mongoClient.close()
  }


  ```

  $search with $$SEARCH_META

  ```kotlin
  import com.mongodb.kotlin.client.coroutine.MongoClient
  import kotlinx.coroutines.flow.collect
  import kotlinx.coroutines.runBlocking
  import org.bson.Document
  import java.time.Instant
  import java.util.Date

  fun main() {
      // Connection URI for your MongoDB Atlas cluster
      val uri = "<connection-string>"
      val mongoClient = MongoClient.create(uri)

      // Set namespace (database and collection)
      val database = mongoClient.getDatabase("sample_mflix")
      val collection = database.getCollection<Document>("movies")

      runBlocking {
          // Define the aggregation pipeline
          val searchStage = Document("\$search", Document("index", "facet-tutorial")
              .append("facet", Document("operator", Document("near", Document("path", "released")
                  // Replace +00:00 with Z
                  .append("origin", Date.from(Instant.parse("1921-11-01T00:00:00.000Z"))) 
                  .append("pivot", 7776000000L)))
                  .append("facets", Document("genresFacet", Document("type", "string")
                      .append("path", "genres"))
                      .append("yearFacet", Document("type", "number").append("path", "year")
                          .append("boundaries", listOf(1910, 1920, 1930, 1940))))))

          val facetStage = Document("\$facet", Document("meta", listOf(
              Document("\$replaceWith", "\$\$SEARCH_META"),
              Document("\$limit", 1)
          )))

          val setStage = Document("\$set", Document("meta", Document("\$arrayElemAt", listOf("\$meta", 0))))

          // Run the aggregation pipeline and print results
          val resultsFlow = collection.aggregate<Document>(
              listOf(searchStage, facetStage, setStage)
          )

          resultsFlow.collect { println(it.toJson()) } // Convert each result to JSON and print
      }

      // Close the MongoDB client
      mongoClient.close()
  }

  ```

- Before you run the sample, replace `<connection-string>` with your
  Atlas connection string. Ensure that your connection string
  includes your database user's credentials. To learn more, see
  [Driver](connect-via-driver).

When you run the `FacetQuery.kt` program in your IDE, it prints
a result similar to the following:

```none
{"meta": {
   "count": {"lowerBound": 20878},
   "facet": {
     "genresFacet": {
       "buckets": [
         {"_id": "Drama", "count": 12149},
         {"_id": "Comedy", "count": 6436},
         {"_id": "Romance", "count": 3274},
         {"_id": "Crime", "count": 2429},
         {"_id": "Thriller", "count": 2400},
         {"_id": "Action", "count": 2349},
         {"_id": "Adventure", "count": 1876},
         {"_id": "Documentary", "count": 1755},
         {"_id": "Horror", "count": 1432},
         {"_id": "Biography", "count": 1244}
       ]
     },
     "yearFacet": {
       "buckets": [
         {"_id": 1910, "count": 14},
         {"_id": 1920, "count": 47},
         {"_id": 1930, "count": 238}
       ]
     }
   }
 }}
 }
}
```

The results show metadata results for two types of facet search. The
`genresFacet` document shows the number of movies in each genre and
the `yearFacet` document shows a count of the number of movies
within the boundaries:

- `1910-1920`, inclusive lower bound for the `1910` bucket and exclusive upper bound for the `1920` bucket

- `1920-1930`, inclusive lower bound for the `1920` bucket and exclusive
  upper bound for the `1930` bucket

- `1930-1940`, inclusive lower bound for the `1930` bucket and exclusive
  upper bound for the `1940` bucket

You can learn more about the [faceted navigation](fts-facet-ref) in MongoDB Search with the following
course or video.

sections.body:
Follow along with this video to learn about how you can create and use a
numeric and string [faceted navigation](fts-facet-ref) in your query to group results
and retrieve a count of the results in the groups.

*Duration: 11 Minutes*

[https://youtu.be/90icEZ8fzJE](https://youtu.be/90icEZ8fzJE)

This tutorial describes how to create an index with a [facet definition](fts-facet-definition) on string, date, and numeric fields
in the `sample_mflix.movies` collection. It shows how to run a MongoDB Search
query against those fields for results grouped by values for the string
field and by ranges for the date and numeric fields, including the
count for each of those groups. It takes you through the following
steps:

- Set up a MongoDB Search index with facet definition on the `genres`,
  `released`, and `year` fields in the `sample_mflix.movies`
  collection.

- Run a MongoDB Search query against the `released` field in the
  `sample_mflix.movies` collection for results grouped by values for
  the `genres` field and by ranges for the `year` field.

To complete these tutorials, in addition to the prerequisites listed in
the [Prerequisites](fts-design-patterns-prereqs), you must have a MongoDB
instance running MongoDB 7.0 or later.

In this section, you will create a MongoDB Search index on the `genres`,
`year`, and `released` fields in the `sample_mflix.movies`
collection.

sections.heading: Step 2: Define the index.
sections.body:
Create a `create_index.py` file in your project directory,
and copy and paste the following code into the file.

```python
from pymongo import MongoClient
from pymongo.operations import SearchIndexModel

# connect to your Atlas deployment
uri = "<connection-string>"
client = MongoClient(uri)

# set namespace
database = client["sample_mflix"]
collection = database["movies"]

# define your MongoDB Search index
search_index_model = SearchIndexModel(
    definition={
        "mappings": {
            "dynamic": True,
            "fields": {
                "genres": {
                    "type": "token"
                }
            }
        },
    },
    name="facet-tutorial",
)

# create the index
result = collection.create_search_index(model=search_index_model)
print(f"New index name: {result}")
```

Replace `<connection-string>` with the connection string for your
Atlas cluster or local Atlas deployment.

Atlas Cluster

Your connection string should use the following format:

```
mongodb+srv://<db_username>:<db_password>@<clusterName>.<hostname>.mongodb.net
```

To learn more, see [Driver](connect-via-driver).

Local or Self-Managed

Your connection string should use the
following format:

```
mongodb://localhost:<port-number>/?directConnection=true
```

To learn more, see [Connection Strings](manual/reference/connection-string).
